title: The Propofol Problem: Why This Non-Controlled Substance Still Demands Your Attention
location: Drug Diversion Insights with Terri Vidals
channelName: Drug Diversion Insights with Terri Vidals
channelDescription:
Drug diversion is a real concern in the healthcare setting. It is estimated that 10% of nurses are dependent on some type of drug. Hospital staff are as vulnerable as the rest of the population to addiction. When you consider that vulnerability, and add to it the access healthcare providers have to the addictive medications, steps must be put in place to monitor for diversion. Allowing a healthcare provider to divert medications from the intended path (the patient) to themselves results in multiple points of risk.

Our pharmacy consultants at Rxpert Solutions can help you review your risk and recommend solutions. Rxpert Solutions can also assist you in correcting deficiencies identified during a survey.

text:
Propofol may not be a federally controlled substance—but that doesn’t mean it’s low risk.

In this Diversion Insights Quick Take from Rxpert Solutions, we break down why propofol continues to be one of the most challenging medications from a diversion prevention and security standpoint. From inconsistent state scheduling to real-world storage and waste disposal challenges, propofol creates vulnerabilities that every healthcare facility must address.

💡 In this episode, we explore:
Why propofol’s non-controlled status creates a false sense of security
The three U.S. states that do classify propofol as a controlled substance
Storage and access challenges that increase diversion risk
Waste disposal practices that leave facilities exposed
Why every organization—regardless of state law—needs a clear propofol strategy

Whether you’re a pharmacy leader, compliance officer, nurse leader, or part of a diversion prevention team, this quick discussion highlights why propofol deserves serious attention—and practical action.

📌 Short, focused, and essential—because some of the biggest risks hide in regulatory gray areas.

🔗 More from Rxpert Solutions

text:
In this episode of Drug Diversion Insights, I’m joined again by Dr. Don Teater, a nationally recognized pain and addiction specialist, to take a deeper look at buprenorphine—from both a clinical and neuroscience perspective.

Building on our previous conversations around pain management, addiction stigma, and trauma, this discussion explores what actually happens in the brain after opioid exposure and how those changes influence treatment decisions.

We dive into complex, real-world questions that healthcare professionals face every day:
• What happens to the brain after opioid use—and how long do those changes last?
• How do responses differ between individuals with and without substance use disorder?
• When should clinicians consider abstinence-based treatment vs. buprenorphine?
• Can healthcare professionals safely return to work while on buprenorphine maintenance?
• What are the risks and considerations after completing abstinence-based recovery?

We also explore emerging research on buprenorphine in postoperative pain management, including:
• Its improved safety profile compared to traditional opioids
• Challenges with rescue dosing
• Considerations for high-tolerance patients

This episode provides valuable insights for opioid stewardship committees, pharmacy leaders, clinicians, and healthcare administrators navigating complex decisions around pain management and substance use disorder treatment.

Whether you’re involved in clinical decision-making, policy development, or patient care, this conversation offers a balanced, evidence-based perspective on one of the most important medications in addiction medicine today.

text:
How many times have you reviewed an anesthesia controlled substance discrepancy report and asked yourself: Is this just a documentation issue—or something more?

In this episode of Drug Diversion Insights, I’m joined by Dr. Jenny Dolan, Division Chief of Pediatric Anesthesia and Director of Trauma Anesthesia at Johns Hopkins All Children’s Hospital, to discuss how her team tackled this exact challenge—and transformed their results.

Starting with 42 discrepancies per month and a 3% error rate, Dr. Dolan led a multi-year quality improvement (QI) initiative that addressed common gaps such as undocumented waste and medication removals without administration—issues that can obscure true diversion risk.

🎯 In this episode, we cover:
• The most common causes of anesthesia discrepancies
• The risks of accepting discrepancies as “normal”
• A step-by-step look at the seven phased interventions
• Why Theory of Change was used as the guiding framework
• Strategies to gain buy-in from anesthesiologists, pharmacists, and informatics teams
• What sustainable improvement actually looks like in practice
• Key insights for OR teams, pharmacy, and healthcare leadership

This conversation provides a practical roadmap for healthcare organizations looking to strengthen controlled substance accountability in anesthesia settings and build stronger interdisciplinary collaboration.

📌 If you’re working to reduce discrepancies, improve audit clarity, or enhance diversion monitoring, this episode offers actionable strategies you can apply today.

🔗 More from Rxpert Solutions

text:
Drug diversion investigations in healthcare are among the most sensitive interviews an investigator can conduct. Get it wrong, and the consequences can be devastating—not just for the investigation, but for a healthcare professional’s career, license, and livelihood.

In this episode of Rxpert Solutions, I’m joined by Dave Thompson, former head of diversion investigations for a large retail pharmacy chain and now President of Wicklander-Zulawski & Associates. Dave has spent years refining how diversion interviews should be conducted—and why many traditional approaches fail.

Old-school tactics like pressure, corner-backing, and presuming guilt often backfire. Drawing on collaboration with researchers and defense attorneys, Dave uses a different approach—one that is trauma-informed, transparent, and focused on truth rather than confessions.

🎯 In this conversation, we explore:
Why diversion interviews carry uniquely high stakes
How poor interview techniques can derail investigations and harm innocent professionals
The role of transparency and fairness in eliciting reliable information
What investigators should understand about stress, fear, and trauma in these settings
How to balance accountability with humanity during diversion inquiries

This episode places Dave in the teacher’s seat, offering practical insight for anyone involved in diversion investigations, compliance, HR, pharmacy leadership, nursing leadership, or healthcare administration.

📌 When careers hang in the balance, how we ask questions matters just as much as the answers we seek.

🔗 More from:
Dave Thompson   / davethompsoncfi   &   / wicklanderzulawski  
Rxpert Solutions: https://www.rxpert.solutions/?utm_sou...

text:
We are thrilled to welcome back John Hertig, PharmD, MS, FASHP and Les Louden, PharmD for another deep dive into the growing body of research on controlled substance waste.

This episode highlights their newest publication:
📄 “Assessing Pharmacy Costs of Intravenous Push Controlled Substance Waste in Hospital-Based Areas: A Multi-Site Study”
Published in Pharmacy (2025): https://www.mdpi.com/2226-4787/13/5/121

What makes this study groundbreaking?
For the first time, researchers quantify the pharmacy workforce costs associated with controlled substance waste — an often overlooked but critically important dimension of medication waste in hospital settings. This is John’s third major study in this space and the second collaboration between John and Les, building on years of research that exposes the true operational and financial impact of waste.

If you haven’t yet, check out our previous episodes in this series:
   • Confronting the Hidden Costs of Medication...  
   • Results of a Workflow Study Time of Contro...   

Together, these studies paint one of the most complete pictures available today of how medication waste affects pharmacy staffing, workflow, costs, and patient safety across the country.

💡 Thanks to our sponsor: MIDAS Healthcare Solutions
Learn how MidasVIEW™ supports safe, compliant controlled substance waste management: https://midashs.com/

More from Rxpert Solutions:

text:
Should a pharmacist or a nurse lead a drug diversion program?
It’s a question that comes up often—and one that deserves a more honest, nuanced conversation.

In this episode of Rxpert Solutions, we dig into what actually matters in diversion program leadership, beyond job titles and professional licenses. The conversation was sparked by a comment from a seasoned diversion specialist who shared that, as a nurse, her qualifications to lead were sometimes questioned. That moment made it clear this topic needed to be addressed openly.

I’m joined by three experienced diversion mitigation leaders:
Bethanie Gamble, with a pharmacy background
Mary Nelson, RN
Alecia Pittman, RN

Together, we bring different professional paths—but a shared commitment to effective diversion oversight. This conversation explores credentials versus competence, and the real skills required to lead complex, high-stakes diversion programs.

🎯 In this episode, we discuss:
What truly qualifies someone to lead a diversion program
The unique strengths nurses and pharmacists bring to the role
The emotional and ethical challenges of diversion leadership
Navigating disagreement with stakeholders when the stakes are high
Lessons that only come from real-world experience

This is an honest, thoughtful discussion for anyone involved in diversion prevention, compliance, pharmacy leadership, nursing leadership, or patient safety. Because effective diversion leadership isn’t about the letters after your name—it’s about judgment, courage, communication, and experience.

🔗 More from Rxpert Solutions: https://www.rxpert.solutions/?utm_sou...

text:
Controlled substance drips and PCA infusions require meticulous oversight—but one of the most common vulnerabilities in medication security occurs during handoffs.

In this Diversion Insights Quick Take, we explore why consistent chain-of-custody practices for controlled substance (CS) drips and PCAs are critical during nurse-to-nurse shift changes and department transfers.

Poor handoff processes create blind spots that can obscure diversion risk while also exposing innocent staff to unnecessary scrutiny.

In this episode, we cover:
• Why volume checks and dual sign-offs matter
• The importance of maintaining a clear chain of custody
• How inconsistent handoffs create audit gaps
• Why proper documentation protects both patients and staff
• Practical steps to strengthen handoff protocols for CS drips and PCAs

For nurses, diversion prevention teams, pharmacy leaders, and healthcare administrators, tightening these practices can dramatically improve both patient safety and diversion detection.

Strong diversion mitigation starts with clear, consistent processes—and every handoff counts.

text:
What does addiction look like when it’s hidden behind a trusted white coat?

In this powerful episode of Drug Diversion Insights, pharmacist Steve Leuck shares his personal journey through cocaine and opioid addiction while actively practicing in a high-trust healthcare environment. For seven years, Steve navigated systems designed to protect patients—while concealing his struggles from colleagues, leadership, and even those closest to him.

This candid conversation explores not only the reality of addiction in healthcare, but also the critical need to build a culture of self-reporting—where professionals feel safe asking for help before harm occurs.

🎯 In this episode, we cover:
• What fueled addiction behind the scenes
• How substance use can remain hidden in healthcare settings
• The warning signs that are often missed
• The intervention that changed everything
• Why stigma prevents early reporting and support
• How organizations can foster a culture of psychological safety and accountability

This episode is essential for healthcare professionals, pharmacy leaders, compliance teams, and anyone involved in diversion prevention or workforce wellbeing.

📌 Because real prevention doesn’t start with detection—it starts with creating an environment where people can come forward safely.

🔗 More from Rxpert Solutions

text:
In the fast-paced and often chaotic world of healthcare, poor medication administration practices can create so much noise that true drug diversion becomes nearly impossible to identify. When shortcuts become routine, accountability breaks down—and risk increases.

In this Diversion Insights Quick Take, we focus on why getting the basics of controlled substance administration right is foundational to effective diversion mitigation. Setting good practice as a non-negotiable expectation clears the fog and allows diversion teams to see real risk signals instead of chasing documentation errors.

💡 In this episode, we cover:
How poor med admin practices erode safety and accountability
Why policies and procedures are your anchor in diversion prevention
The role of targeted education in strengthening compliance
How strong fundamentals lead to cleaner audits and better investigations

Whether you’re a nurse, pharmacist, diversion analyst, or compliance leader, this short episode reinforces why diversion mitigation starts with consistent, reliable medication administration practices.

📌 Because you can’t identify diversion when basic practice is optional.

text:
Few topics in addiction medicine generate as much debate as buprenorphine. In this episode of Drug Diversion Insights, we address one of the most controversial subjects raised by listeners: the real-world experience of being on—and coming off—buprenorphine.

After receiving numerous messages from individuals describing how poorly they felt on the medication and how difficult discontinuation was, Terri sits down with returning guest Dr. Stuart Gitlow to explore this issue from both a scientific and patient-centered perspective.

Together, we discuss:
• The science behind medication for opioid use disorder (MOUD)
• Why buprenorphine works for many patients
• Reported challenges with discontinuation
• Post-acute withdrawal symptoms (PAWS) and long-term effects
• What patients should understand before starting treatment
• The gap between clinical messaging and lived experience

This candid, evidence-based conversation does not dismiss patient experiences — nor does it ignore the life-saving role buprenorphine has played in opioid use disorder treatment. Instead, it aims to provide clarity, context, and balanced insight into one of the most widely used — and often misunderstood — medications in recovery.

Whether you are a healthcare provider, patient, policymaker, or someone interested in addiction medicine, this episode offers critical perspective on efficacy, expectations, and informed decision-making.

title: Anticipatory Compounding: Navigating the FDA-DEA Divide in Hospital Pharmacies
channelName: Drug Diversion Insights with Terri Vidals
channelDescription:
Drug diversion is a real concern in the healthcare setting. It is estimated that 10% of nurses are dependent on some type of drug. Hospital staff are as vulnerable as the rest of the population to addiction. When you consider that vulnerability, and add to it the access healthcare providers have to the addictive medications, steps must be put in place to monitor for diversion. Allowing a healthcare provider to divert medications from the intended path (the patient) to themselves results in multiple points of risk.

text:
In this Quick Take episode of Rxpert Solutions, we tackle a regulatory gray area that doesn’t get enough attention: anticipatory compounding of controlled substances.

While batching IVs ahead of time seems like a smart efficiency move, it sits at a complex intersection where FDA and DEA regulations don’t always align.

💡 In this episode, you’ll learn:
The key differences between FDA and DEA perspectives on anticipatory compounding
Why this issue matters for sterile compounding and controlled substance handling
What hospital pharmacies must document to remain compliant
Practical guidance for balancing operational efficiency with regulatory expectations

Whether you’re a pharmacy director, compliance officer, or sterile compounding manager, this short, actionable discussion will help you navigate this tricky compliance challenge and protect your facility from regulatory risk.

📌 Brief, focused, and practical—because not every compliance topic needs an hour-long deep dive.

text:
Think you need expensive surveillance software to detect drug diversion? Think again.

In this Diversion Insights Quick Take, we break down how manual auditing using ADM data can be a powerful and practical way to identify risk early—no advanced tools required.

Whether you’re building a diversion monitoring program from scratch or strengthening an existing one, this episode focuses on doing something effective rather than waiting for the “perfect” system.

🎯 In this episode, we cover:
• How to select meaningful audit targets without software
• Why comparing “like nurses with like nurses” matters
• Identifying outliers in dispensing and waste patterns
• Why cancel transactions on non-controlled meds can be key red flags
• Practical, no-frills strategies to start auditing today

Manual audits may be simple—but when done correctly, they can uncover patterns that automated systems might miss.

This episode is ideal for nurses, pharmacists, diversion specialists, compliance leaders, and healthcare administrators looking to improve accountability and patient safety without relying on expensive tools.

📌 You don’t need perfect technology to make progress—just the right approach.

title: Securing the Unsecured: A Mayo Clinic Study on Propofol Disposal and Diversion Mitigation
location: Drug Diversion Insights with Terri Vidals
channelName: Drug Diversion Insights with Terri Vidals
channelDescription:
Drug diversion is a real concern in the healthcare setting. It is estimated that 10% of nurses are dependent on some type of drug. Hospital staff are as vulnerable as the rest of the population to addiction. Our pharmacy consultants at Rxpert Solutions can help you review your risk and recommend solutions. Rxpert Solutions can also assist you in correcting deficiencies identified during a survey.
aboutChannelInfo.channelName: Drug Diversion Insights with Terri Vidals

text:
Propofol remains one of the most challenging medications to secure in healthcare settings — not because it lacks abuse potential, but because it lacks the regulatory oversight applied to controlled substances.

In this episode of Rxpert Solutions, I’m joined by Michael T. Ring and Dale M. Pfrimmer to discuss their impactful study published in Critical Care Nurse:
📄 “Propofol as a Drug of Diversion: Changing Disposal Practices to Reduce Risk.” 

Their research tackles a major, often overlooked vulnerability in ICUs: unsecured propofol waste. Traditional practices like pouring excess medication down the sink or tossing full bottles into open trash bins create easy diversion opportunities — and increase environmental harm.

💡 What their Mayo Clinic team did differently:
Implemented activated carbon disposal pouches (Deterra System)
Introduced custom bottle-opening tools
Educated staff and aligned workflows
Reduced full, recoverable propofol bottles in waste bins from 44.1% → 0%

This episode explores their methodology, sustainability strategies, and how their success expanded across all Mayo Clinic ICUs and the emergency department.

You’ll hear practical insights about:
Staff concerns around diversion risk
Embedding compliance into workflow design
Environmental responsibility in medication disposal
Creative interventions that protect both patients and healthcare workers

This study is a powerful reminder that with the right tools and strategy, we can secure high-risk medications—even without traditional controlled-substance regulations.

aboutChannelInfo.channelName: Drug Diversion Insights with Terri Vidals

text:
The most advanced diversion monitoring tools in the world won’t work if staff don’t trust the culture surrounding them.

In this Diversion Insights Quick Take, we explore one of the most overlooked aspects of diversion prevention: staff engagement. When healthcare professionals see diversion mitigation as a system designed to protect patients and support colleagues—not simply punish people—they become far more willing to speak up, report concerns early, and participate in creating a safer environment.

🎯 In this episode, we discuss:
• Why culture matters more than surveillance alone
• How fear and stigma discourage early reporting
• The importance of psychological safety in healthcare
• Why early identification of substance use disorder protects both patients and staff
• How supportive diversion programs improve engagement and accountability
• Practical ways to shift from a punitive mindset to a prevention-focused culture

This episode is essential for healthcare leaders, diversion specialists, pharmacists, nurses, compliance teams, and patient safety professionals looking to strengthen diversion prevention through trust, communication, and staff partnership.

📌 Because your greatest defense against diversion isn’t technology alone—it’s a workforce that feels safe enough to speak up.

text:
Controlled substance waste is one of the most common—and most overlooked—vulnerabilities in diversion prevention.

Delayed wasting, incomplete witnessing, and inconsistent processes create opportunities for tampering and introduce doubt during audits. When waste practices aren’t tight, even well-intentioned staff can be placed under unnecessary scrutiny.

In this Diversion Insights Quick Take, we break down why integrated waste at dispense is considered the gold standard for controlled substances—and how it helps eliminate risk at one of the most critical points in the medication-use process.

🎯 In this episode, we cover:
• Why delayed wasting creates diversion risk
• The dangers of unwitnessed or poorly documented waste
• What “integrated waste” means and why it works
• Common pitfalls that weaken accountability
• Practical steps to strengthen waste protocols and audit readiness

This episode is essential for nurses, pharmacists, diversion specialists, compliance leaders, and healthcare administrators looking to close gaps, improve documentation, and build stronger, more defensible processes.

📌 Because when waste isn’t handled correctly, it becomes a blind spot for both safety and accountability.
